title: Ser vs Estar

primarySong.title: Estoy Aquí
primarySong.note: Final song locked by team-lead 2026-05-18 redirect. Title IS the lesson — 'Estoy Aquí' uses estar twice (state + location) in two words. Pop and R&B genre tracks both reuse the primary; reggaeton = 'Calma' (Pedro Capó & Farruko); regional Mexican = 'El Rey' (Vicente Fernández).

notes: Module 6 audio set — narration-first per Reine's vision (locked 2026-05-15). Scripts lifted from module-006-content.ts.draft audioTextManifest (post-redirect — anchored to Shakira / Estoy Aquí, replacing earlier Marc Anthony draft). M5 gold-standard style — full English sentences with Spanish tokens embedded, single MP3 per cue, no slow/normal pairs. 9 narration cues + 8 reading-token cues = 17 files total. Speed-rule fix from prior revision: m6-reading-001 and m6-cultural-note-reggaeton flipped from 0.95 to 1.0 (both are 4+ word texts — same artifact-avoidance reason as M3 donde-jugaran). Reading-token speed rule: single Spanish word → 0.9, 2-word phrases ('estoy aquí', 'pies descalzos') → 0.95. Reading-token text matches passage tappableTerms exactly (case + accents).

ttsTriggers.text: Today we're learning the two Spanish verbs for 'to be' — ser and estar. Ser is for who someone is. Estar is for how they are or where they are. As you listen to 'Estoy Aquí' by Shakira, count how many times she sings estoy. That's estar — the verb of the moment. Then count the soy moments — that's ser, the verb of identity. Press listen when you're ready.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, teacherly — sets up the lesson, primes the song
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Mixed-language opener. nova reads English as American English, Spanish tokens (ser, estar, soy, estoy, Estoy Aquí, Shakira) with Spanish phonemes. 'Estoy Aquí' (the song title) must sound like the Spanish title — es-TOY a-KEE, both words clearly Spanish, NOT Anglicized. 'Shakira' (sha-KEE-ra) is her stage name as commonly pronounced. The repeated 'soy' and 'estoy' tokens must be clean Spanish (pure vowels, no English drift). Estimated duration: 30s.

ttsTriggers.text: Ser is the 'to be' for identity — who someone is. Soy means 'I am'. Eres means 'you are'. Es means 'he is' or 'she is'. Use these when you're naming a person, a profession, a nationality — anything that doesn't change overnight.
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Three conjugations to pronounce cleanly: Soy (one syllable, pure 'soy'), Eres (EH-res, single-tap r — Module 1 pronunciation rule, NOT a Castilian rolled rr), Es (single short syllable, soft 's'). The infinitive 'Ser' opens — should sound like Spanish 'sair', not English 'sur'. Estimated duration: 16s.

ttsTriggers.text: Estar is the 'to be' for states and locations — how someone is or where they are. Estoy means 'I am'. Estás means 'you are'. Está means 'he is' or 'she is'. Use these for feelings, mood, and where someone is standing right now. When Shakira sings estoy aquí, that's estar twice — the state she's in, and where she is.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: clear, instructive — teach-the-conjugation tone with song anchor
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: MOST IMPORTANT QA ITEM IN THE MODULE. The two-syllable es-TAR / es-TOY / es-TÁS / es-TÁ pattern must stress the SECOND syllable — that's where the verb identity lives. The accent marks on Estás (es-TÁS) and Está (es-TÁ) tell nova to stress the final syllable. The closing 'estoy aquí' (es-TOY a-KEE) is the song title in mini — must sound like the title, anchoring the lesson to Shakira's song. QA listen: does 'Está' (with accent) sound clearly stressed on the final syllable, distinct from 'esta' (the demonstrative)? Estimated duration: 20s.

ttsTriggers.text: Here's the trick. 'Soy feliz' and 'estoy feliz' both translate as 'I am happy' in English — but they mean different things in Spanish. Soy feliz says you're a happy person by nature. Estoy feliz says you're happy in this moment. Same English, two truths.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: clear, instructive — the conceptual crux of the lesson
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: The four 'feliz' occurrences must sound consistent — feh-LEES with stress on the second syllable and a soft final 's'. The contrast pair (Soy feliz / Estoy feliz) appears TWICE in this cue — once introducing the puzzle, once resolving it — and both pronunciations must match so the minimal-pair teaching lands cleanly. Estimated duration: 18s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'Estoy bien, ¿y tú?' — I'm well, and you? This is the textbook answer to ¿Cómo estás? from Module 3. Estoy is the form of estar — it's about how you feel right now. Then y tú — and you? Memorize the whole chunk. It's the single most useful exchange you'll leave today's lesson with.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, conversational — modeling a real exchange the learner can use
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: The opening 'Estoy bien, ¿y tú?' is the learner's takeaway exchange — it MUST sound like a natural conversational reply, not a flat recitation. The question intonation on '¿y tú?' should rise naturally (Module 3 recycling). 'Bien' is one syllable, said quickly: bee-EN compressed. '¿Cómo estás?' embedded in the middle should also lift in pitch at the end. Estimated duration: 19s.

ttsTriggers.text: Try this with me. Look in a mirror. First, a ser sentence — 'Soy', plus your name. That's who you are. Now an estar sentence — 'Estoy bien', or 'estoy cansado', or 'estoy aquí'. That's how you are right now, or where you are. Same person, two verbs, two different things said out loud.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, encouraging — coaching the learner to actually speak
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Instructional rhythm matters — there should be a clear pause after 'plus your name' and 'right now, or where you are' so the learner has space to look at themselves and speak. The three state-sentence options (Estoy bien / estoy cansado / estoy aquí) need clean separation. 'Cansado' is kan-SAH-do, masculine ending. 'Estoy aquí' must match the song-title pronunciation from other cues. Estimated duration: 19s.

ttsTriggers.text: Shakira was born in Barranquilla, on Colombia's Caribbean coast, in 1977. By the time she was eighteen, she had already signed with Sony Music and recorded the album that would carry her out of Colombia and into the rest of Latin America — Pies Descalzos, released in 1995. The first single was a song with a title that, by itself, teaches you today's whole lesson. Estoy aquí — I am here. Two words, two verbs of estar doing two jobs at once: estoy is the state she's in, and aquí anchors her to the place. Notice what she did not sing. She did not sing soy aquí. Spanish would not accept it. Soy is for identity, and a place is not who you are. When she tells someone he eres something to her, that's ser. When she tells you how she está in this moment, that's estar. The whole song is a lesson in two verbs.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — narration tone for the artist/cultural bio
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: SPEED FIX: was 0.95 in prior revision — flipped to 1.0 per speed rule (4+ word text MUST be ≥1.0 to avoid the donde-jugaran stretching artifact from M3). Spanish proper nouns must be pronounced cleanly: Shakira (sha-KEE-ra), Barranquilla (ba-rran-KEE-ya — soft Latin-American 'll'), Colombia (ko-LOM-bia), Sony Music (English, as the company name), Pies Descalzos (PYES des-CAL-sos — the album title), Estoy aquí (es-TOY a-KEE — the song title; appears twice in narration, must sound identical both times). The four 'soy' / 'soy aquí' / 'eres' / 'está' tokens scattered through the passage must be clean Spanish. The 'She did not sing soy aquí' line is a teaching moment — 'soy aquí' should sound deliberately wrong-but-grammatically-Spanish, not garbled. Estimated duration: ~59s at 1.0x.

ttsTriggers.text: If you picked reggaeton, your song today is 'Calma' — the remix by Pedro Capó and Farruko, both Puerto Rican artists. The track became one of the most-streamed Spanish-language songs of the late 2010s, and like Shakira's 'Estoy Aquí', it leans hard on estar. Pedro Capó sings about being on the beach, being at peace, being in the moment — present-state descriptions that all call for estoy or está. Listen for estoy and es in the chorus. That's estar telling you where he is, and ser telling you what the day is. Two verbs, one slower-tempo reggaeton, the same lesson.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — narration tone, pair-toned with the reading passage
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: SPEED FIX: was 0.95 in prior revision — flipped to 1.0 per speed rule (4+ word text MUST be ≥1.0). CONDITIONAL cue — only plays when user.genre === 'reggaeton'. Spanish-token QA: 'Calma' (CAHL-ma — single-tap l, soft m, Spanish 'a' vowels), 'Pedro Capó' (PEH-dro ca-PÓ — accent on the final ó, stressed), 'Farruko' (fa-RRU-ko — doubled R is fine if rolled), 'Estoy Aquí' (Spanish, consistent with the reading passage and other cues). 'Puerto Rican' English. Stretch goal — generate if budget permits, otherwise defer to a later batch. Estimated duration: ~30s.

ttsTriggers.text: You can now say who you are AND how you are. Two verbs, two truths. Ser for identity — soy, eres, es. Estar for state and location — estoy, estás, está. The same English 'I am' splits into two Spanish jobs, and you have both. Try one mirror moment tomorrow — 'Soy' plus your name, then 'Estoy' plus how you feel. Same person, two verbs.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, celebratory — closing the loop, looking forward
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Closing cue — should feel like a coach giving a final pep talk, not a reading. The two three-form roll-calls (soy / eres / es AND estoy / estás / está) should be paced with brief beats between forms — comma rhythm, not rushed. The accent contrast está/estás vs the unaccented forms must come through audibly. Estimated duration: 22s.
